sentences.text: Yes?
OK.
They will not make noise.
But I-- when I--
Yeah, OK, OK.
Yeah.
OK.
My name is Margot Schlesinger.
M-A-R-G-O-T is my first name.
And my last name is S-C-H-L-E-S-I-N-G-E-R.
On my birth certificate, I had Maria Miriam Margot.
But I only use Margot.
My last name is Schlesinger.
I had only one name.
Wind was my maiden name.
W-I-N-D.
My birth date is the 24th of July, 1918.
I am now 76 years and a half, I guess.
In Berlin.
Berlin, Germany.
My home.
I had four brothers and two sisters, and I had a very loving family.
My parents were wonderful.
My father was very religious.

My mother was a little bit more modern, and she attended all our-- whenever we had something, like we played theater, or she was there when we-- and she always wanted us children should have everything.
Like, we should be educated.
She was very much for education.
My father didn't care so much for the girls, but for boys, he wanted education too.
They're very good students, my brothers.
They went to a-- first they went to a public school, and then they went to a day school.
And so was I.
I went to a day school, and my sister went to a day school.
We all were very much Hebrew educated and-- and German, of course.
You know, we lived in Germany.
I didn't-- when my parents wanted us not to understand something, they spoke sometimes in Polish.
[LAUGHS] Yeah.
What ages?
I started when I was-- I-- I right way started the day school.
So I was about 5 and 1/2, 6 years old, until I graduated eight grades, 14-- I was 14 years old.
And then I-- then I go-- went to a kindergarten, like, to a-- to-- wanted to be a kindergartner.
But of course, you know, I couldn't go on.
Because of 1933, Hitler came in.
And so I had to go and find a job in a Jewish company.
I could not work on Saturdays in [INAUDIBLE].
You know?
So I found a very nice job where we designed lingerie and beachwear and pajamas.
And I was cutting them, and I learned to design them.
So I had a very good job.
I worked there for six years.
Tell us a little bit more about your father's connection to Judaism.
What was--
My father's connection to Judaism was he was a [NON-ENGLISH], which means he was a scholar of Talmud, and he even learned Kabbalah, Kabbalah, which is very high-- I mean, not everybody can do that.
So a lot of people came to him.
He belonged to a shul.
There were three shuls in the Jewish neighborhood where he belonged to one of the shuls.
And they were sitting, learning a lot.
And then, you know, he had to make a living.
So he sold like linens and all kinds of things.
We had, a little-- like, a little linen store or something my parents owned.
That's--
My mother helped too, yeah.
My mother-- with six children, she had to help.
Because we had help in the house.
With six children, she couldn't do it herself.
So she helped all the time to sell and things like that, yeah.
And she-- and there were no washing machines and no driers.
She had to bake and cook and everything too herself.
I mean, we had somebody there, a maid, but it wasn't enough.
Wonderful.
We had wonderful holidays.
We always looked forward for Passover.
I-- I-- I never forget.
I mean, Passover, when we came home from school, everything was upside down.
All the boxes were there, and then we couldn't eat in one of the rooms which were already clean.
So we had to-- we had to eat in the hall outside.
My parents were very strict about that.
We were-- my-- we were very-- I mean, my parents were religious minded.
My mother was a little bit more modern.
But she had to do everything my father wanted to do it the way he wanted it.
Oh yeah.
I went-- first of all, I belonged to-- it was a-- like a group where we went on tours.
It was like-- [NON-ENGLISH] it was called.
You know, [NON-ENGLISH] is a trumpeter was at that time.
We wanted to belong to something which was already connected with Israel.
And then I belonged to [NON-ENGLISH] This was a sport club.
I played handball.
And even in our school, I was very much with swimming and things like that.
You know, before the war, we were very much-- and we played, like I say, we played theater for the [NON-ENGLISH].
We had one group which we were playing with my brothers.
We played all in theaters and things like this.
There was a Jewish [NON-ENGLISH] in Germany.
Until 1937, it was not so bad yet.
I mean, you know, they did not right away do what they did afterwards and what they wanted to do.
Because it was very systematically-- they did it day by day, and then week by week, everything's changed.
So we were always afraid.
But yet it went on.
We went to concerts.
We went to-- there was a Jewish [NON-ENGLISH], they had concerts.
It was wonderful.
My role was, first of all, I played handball.
And we went to other cities.
We played against others like here, you know?
We played against other cities and against other sport clubs, against [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH], And we were from Berlin.
So we played against each other.
We were-- every Sunday we were.
And it was like, in [NON-ENGLISH], there was a sport-- a very big sport park.
And we came there every Sunday.
And during the week, we were busy with, you know, with working and things like that.
But it was-- was nice until 1937, 1938, it was already a little bit different.
And in 1938, I don't know whether you heard about the Kristallnacht.
OK.
Yeah, sure.
Yes.
Yeah.
I tell you, with my brothers and sisters, we had a very good relationship, because my brothers were older than me.
The only problem I had with them is when we went out and somebody wanted to make a date with me, they had a very hard time to-- to accepting that.
They investigated, and always they said to me, don't go out with this guy.
And I heard them talk about girls, so I always felt that if I get into some situation, [INAUDIBLE] about it would be terrible.
So I always was careful with whom I dated too.
So-- and I had a lot of-- I mean, I had a lot of asking to be dated.
So I had a hard time.
I was 16 and 17 years old.
And one time, when I was 16 years old, the [NON-ENGLISH], the leader of one of the group from [INAUDIBLE] who came to play against us in Berlin.
He was from Dresden.
He fell in love with me, and I fell in love with him.
And I was 16 years old.
And my parents were very much against it because he wasn't religious at all.
My father, when he heard that I dated somebody who was not religious at all, he was very much upset, and I got-- of course, you know, my parents were telling me, you cannot do that.
You cannot-- you cannot-- our name is too good.
You can't do that to us.
You know.
So I was-- with this, I had a hard time with, when I wanted to date somebody that wasn't religious.
Dating in Berlin was going out to these sports clubs and events.
When we were winning in the evening, we had, like, a party.
You know, we were dancing, and it was very nice.
We had a very nice time.
We went out to coffee houses, you know, things like that.
And everything was open still.
And Jewish people were not so much yet-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, we were not so much afraid.
And yet we were afraid.
We were afraid of what's coming.
But yet, they let us go on a little bit like people going out and things like that.
Until 1938, it was not so bad yet.
What was so bad, I want you to know, was that the SR, SR, the [NON-ENGLISH] it was called.
These were the young people, like the Hitler youth.
They were marching in front of our-- in our streets.
And they were screaming.
They were singing.
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
That means if the Jewish blood is going from my knife, then it's even better.
Something like this.
And we had to listen to that.
But we were used to it already because my-- our neighbors, they were from one day to the next.
They were wonderful friends to us, and then from one day to the next, one time my brother came home.
My brother Julius, the youngest one, came home, and he had a bleeding nose.
And he said that a neighbor of his, if not for him, he would have been killed, because the SR was after him.
And he came in between, and he said to him, let him be alone because he's a friend of ours.
So he came.
He was OK.
He came home, but he was bleeding from his nose and things like that.
So that's what we had to go through, these little incidents happen.
Yes, we had some Jewish people in our neighborhood.
It was mostly a Jewish neighborhood, but there were some gentiles next to us too.
So--
Our building-- actually, in our building, there were not many Jewish people.
We were most-- we were maybe about three families, and it was a big building.
It was a building on one side-- we lived on the first floor.
And it was-- on one side, was [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], and then the other side was [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
You had to go two.
You could go to both ways-- two entrances.
The apartment, we had, like, two, three bedrooms.
Three bedrooms.
And we had a living room and a kitchen and a washroom and-- one washroom only.
And what else did we have?
This was it.
Modern in what regard?
I mean, modern-- you know, nobody else had different.
We all lived the same way.
I mean, the people I was connected, with.
You know, All my friends and things like that.
In Berlin, we had only one aunt.
One aunt.
And we had two cousins.
That's it.
We didn't have many.
My parents my father, we came in 1911.
And at that time, we didn't have so many relatives.
My father came from Poland, you know.
And he had the Polish passports.
And we had to be under his restriction.
Like, you know, we belonged to him.
And so we were Polish citizen even though we were born in Berlin.
It wasn't like here in America, if you're born in America, you have-- you know, you can have the citizenship either way as an American.
That's what I believe it is.
I don't know.
Is it like that?
Yeah.
Yeah.
OK.
Why he came to Germany.
I believe he came to Germany because there was a war coming up, and he didn't want-- he didn't to-- he didn't want to be in the Army.
Yeah.
He didn't want--
Hm?
And my mother came because she met my father, and she got married to him, and they came both together.
And at that time, when children were born, they had-- they had a hard time-- they didn't-- you know, they didn't register or something like that.
So my brothers, they had to do-- later on, they had to get married again or something in Berlin, after two were already born or so.
But they-- two brothers were born in Poland.
My mother came from-- with the brothers from Poland.
My brother Eddie, who was the oldest one, who was born in 1909.
And then I had one brother right after him.
He was born in 1910.
And one in 1913.
So the two first ones came-- were born in Poland.
So they came with-- with my parents from Poland.
Yes.
My mother went once and took my youngest sister to see her mother in Poland.
This was the only contact I remember.
And I was very upset because she took my youngest sister.
And I stayed home, and I wanted to go and meet my grandmother too.
But she didn't take me.
So she brought me a very nice little ring when she came back.
So that was the prize I got for not going.
Yeah.
While I was growing up, I tell you the truth.
I was always very well liked.
I didn't notice my neighbors, but I knew and I heard about other people who had a lot of problems.
They were broken into and the windows were broken and things like that.
But I cannot really remember until 1937, '38.
I don't remember too much about, you know, incidents other than what my brother.
My brothers, they have different-- you know, they know more than I do because I was younger than them.
So--
The first signs of real danger was in in 1938.
I worked for that company.
And there was, like, Kristallnacht, of course.
And when I came to-- I met a policeman, a German policeman, on the street.
And he told me that I shouldn't walk by myself because they're shooting people and things like that.
So he actually went part with me to my place where I worked.
And when I came there, the windows were broken in, and our company was completely closed.
You know, they closed the company.
And I lost my job in 1938, in October or November it was, yeah.
And then of course, 1938, after that was like-- they took all the Polish people.
My brothers got, like, from somebody who was Turkish, they had a Turkish passport.
And they were telling them that they're going to pick up all the Polish citizens that have the Polish passport.
They're going to pick them up, and they're going to send them to Polish borders.
What's called [NON-ENGLISH].
My brothers went to hide, actually.
They went to this house that had Turkish-- Jewish people living.
And they stayed there for a while until it was over.
They took-- they came to our house, and they wanted-- they were looking.
The police came.
The German police came.
And they were looking for my brothers, and they were not there.
I was there with my brother-- with my mother and with my father.
And my father wasn't taken at that time.
But since my brothers were not home, they came the next morning, 6 o'clock.
My father was davening.
And they took him to the police station.
They didn't send him away.
They kept him at the police station for eight days.
I went every day.
My boss at the time said-- said to me, the company was just liquidating, you know.
The [NON-ENGLISH] came in.
And my boss said to me at the time, you know what?
You can go and find your father.
Maybe you can get him out.
After eight days, I got my father back out.
But they had, anyhow, they were expelled.
They had to leave for Poland afterwards.
But at least they didn't go with the transport, which other people went.
And at that time, they took my sister.
She was in [NON-ENGLISH] in a different town.
In Berlin, they only took the men, at the time, to go to the border to [NON-ENGLISH].
But in Berlin, they took only the men.
So my brothers were not there.
So they came the next morning.
They said, since your sons are not here-- to me-- to my mother and to me, I have to take your husband.
So he took-- they took him to the police station.
He was there for eight days, and I found-- I found where he was.
And I was standing there every day, and I was talking to the police guys.
Finally, they released him.
But we had to leave.
They had to leave anyhow, and I had to leave.
I didn't know where to go.
We were standing in line for days and days.
It was like [INAUDIBLE] the highest, the Jewish [NON-ENGLISH], it was called.
And we were standing there, and we wanted to get some visas or so.
Only one [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one country let people go in [?
Siam. ?] And I had a visa who wanted to go to-- to [?
Siam. ?] But if I didn't have any choice.

So I-- in the meantime, I got a permit to go to England, to go into a hospital to learn to be a nurse or to [? kindergartner. ?] And in the meantime, my parents went to Poland, because they had to go to Poland.
And so I joined them instead.
Because my brothers-- the two brothers got their certificate to go to America, my two oldest brothers.
And my brother Willie had to go in 1936.
He left already for Palestine.
At that time, it was on a British mandate.
So he left for Israel.
And my sister was taken anyhow from-- you know, from the [INAUDIBLE] to Poland.
And then she got her certificate, and she went to Israel.
So I was left with my parents.
And I went to town of where my parents are from.
And we-- we could send a lift.
You know, we had some-- things taken out, the most valuable things, to go to-- to-- my parents rented a small apartment, And we lived there until 1939.
The war broke out.
And then it was hell.
How much time passed between when your parents left for Poland and when you joined them?
Very, very short time.
My parents left in January, and I left in June.
And my brother left-- my brother Heinrich was still in Berlin, and he left to Shanghai with the last ship in 1939. 12 days before the war broke out, my brother Heinrich left.
At that time, I was 20-- 20, 20, yeah.
I wasn't working anymore.
What I did was I learned a lot of English.
I had a friend who was an English teacher, and I learned English.
And I learned also-- I made pattern.
Privately.
I paid for that, and I learned to make pattern and, you know, clothes.
I learned that.
Because this I didn't learn from before.
So I had a chance because I had free time, and we didn't do anything.
My parents had money from-- from Germany which was sent out illegally before-- before the war.
I joined them in Tarnow.
This was 1939.
And we lived there until 1941 or-- yeah.
I lived there until 1942 with my parents.
And in 1942, there was one deportation.
What happened was that the Germans came and the war broke out.
You want to go on with this?
Yeah.
The Germans came in in 1939.
The war broke out the first of September.
And right away, there were restrictions for us.
We had to, like, give up furs, and we give up, like, radios and things like that.
We couldn't hold it.
Everything was under death penalty.
Every morning, you got up, and there was a big, big, like-- how should I call it.
It was called in German, it's a [GERMAN].
And there were [GERMAN].
New-- new-- new laws.
You know, everything was under death penalty.
So we lived that way.
Every morning, there was something else.
My parents were very much afraid.
Until one day, there was a registration.
Everybody had to come to register.
Now, I did not have any Polish papers.
And the Germans would not acknowledge a German paper, and I didn't have anything to go with.
So I went out.
I stayed there for-- it was a terrible, hot day.
It was in June, 1942.
I didn't even let my parents go because it wouldn't do any good.
And my husband at that time was a friend of mine because he was in the same line which my cousin too was.
You know, they had fabrics.
And he knew me.
What happened was he went to the registration and he got two [? seals. ?] He will tell you about that.
This is not my story.
My story is only that when I came home, I was very upset, and I was crying.
And I said, I don't know what's going to be.
I have no-- no paper with.
What they did was they went with all these registrations, whoever was registered, to the Judenrat.
That's a Jewish-- Jewish counsel who worked-- they worked with the Germans together.
So they had the names.
And the first day of the first deportation, this deportation went on for three days.
It was the-- it was the fifth-- the-- I have it marked down here.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
This was the first deportation.
And this was going on-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] June the 11th was the first day.
I stayed with my parents, and nothing happened, thank God, because we were not taken.
We were not on the list.
The second deportation, when they said it will be the second day, it was the 15th, my mother had some earrings, some diamond earrings.
And she had some brooches, some valuables.
And she said to me-- we had friends which were a friend of my husband too.
So she said, maybe you can go to the suburbs and-- and hide them there.
And I went.
And the next morning, I came back, and my husband has already going-- he wanted to help me a little bit.
So he went and he made a paper, marriage paper.
Just a little piece of paper which said that I belong to him.
And I came back, and our apartment was sealed.
What they did is they took my parents out.
They were shooting 10,000 people.
I am sure they were shot at the cemetery.
And 10,000 people were sent to [INAUDIBLE].
So when I came back, I had no place to go.
I had nothing.
The apartment was sealed, and I had nothing.
So my husband offered me to come to his house.
He lived with his brother further out.
So I went to the house where my-- where my husband-- at that time, he was a friend of mine only.
And we-- I stayed there for a while.
Then they opened up our apartment again.
And it became, actually, they made a ghetto out of it later on.
So my husband had to move in then to my apartment, because it became the ghetto.
This was in 1943, after the first deportation.
The first deportation was in three-- in three days.
Then there was another day.
Of course, I had nothing to do with the other day.
But the other day, they came to my husband's house.
And my husband, he-- on his [INAUDIBLE] on his walking paper, he had like-- it said businessman, because he was a businessman.
He had a big business.
And so when he came to the registration, he saw somebody getting a little K, and there was a little-- by the registration, there was a big seal and a small seal.
Now, he saw somebody getting the small seal.
And this guy asked something, so the gestapo hit him over the head.
That's what my husband told me.
So he said, I'm not a business-- oh, he said, you're a businessman.
He wanted to take the small seal to give it to him.
So he said, no I'm not a businessman anymore.
Now I'm a locksmith.
My husband paid a locksmith to get employed by him.
So he had already [INAUDIBLE] with the locksmith.
So when he came to that, he said, I am now a locksmith.
So he took the big seal.
And he had another paper, his identification, with his name and his picture on it and his age on it.
He put that in too, and he gave him two seals.
So he had the two seals.
When I was in my-- in my husband's apartment, it was already the third deportation.
Now, these gestapo people, they came in, and they never-- they never, you know, knocked on the door.
They came with the big boots, and they just knocked the door in or something.
And they were always a little drunk.
They had the bayonet, you know, with-- with them, and the guns.
And they came in, identification.
And I showed them the marriage paper.
I thought that that's fine, you know?
This will keep me.
So he said, you come with me.
Now, this was a big building too.
And everybody who was taken down there in the building, it was screaming all terrible.
Everybody was shot.
And going down the steps.
So I showed him-- when I showed him the marriage licence, he said, that's nice, and you come with me.
And my husband said to me, why don't you show him the Juden pass with his picture and with his age on it.
And I said, here.
This-- this is my Juden pass.
So he shut up, and he said, you can stay.
And I was saved for that time.
But of course, it wasn't the end.
Right after that, there was another deportation, and so on.
You and your husband were still friends at this point?
We were still friends at this point.
As a matter of fact, not only were we friends-- in 1942, after everything, my husband said, we have to get married.
So we-- we were afraid to get married because the gestapo wouldn't allow us to get married.
But was-- we were-- I was like standing in the kitchen, and my husband said, you know, somebody die, and a rabbi's coming in.
And we were like, about eight of nine people.
But outside, there were some people who did not show up for work.
There were 17 people shot in front of our house at the day we were married.
And it wasn't [INAUDIBLE] I mean, it wasn't a ceremony.
It was just, a rabbi gave us, like, [INAUDIBLE], which means he-- he married us.
My husband had a little ring made for me.
And so that was the wedding.
